questions.question: What is the difference between `{{ ... }}` and `{% ... %}` in Liquid?
choices: `{{ ... }}` is for strings, `{% ... %}` is for numbers.
`{{ ... }}` outputs a value into the rendered HTML; `{% ... %}` is a logic tag that produces no output by itself.
`{{ ... }}` is evaluated at build time, `{% ... %}` is evaluated in the browser.
They are interchangeable; the difference is purely stylistic.
questions.explanation: Liquid has exactly two syntactic forms: output tags `{{ }}` emit a value, and logic tags `{% %}` control flow (assigns, loops, conditionals) without emitting output.

questions.question: You need a list of every post that is *not* the page currently being viewed. Which Liquid expression is correct?
questions.explanation: `where` only does equality on a single key. To filter on an arbitrary boolean expression you need `where_exp`, which binds each item to a local name and evaluates the expression.

questions.question: Your template renders `<time>{{ post.date | date: "%-d %b %Y" }}</time>` correctly for `_posts/*.md`, but when you reuse the same snippet on a custom page with `date: "2026-01-15"` in front matter, the filter prints junk. Why?
choices: The `date` filter only works on `post.*` paths.
Liquid `date` requires a Date value; quoted strings in front matter come through as strings unless YAML parses them as dates.
Liquid `date` is locale-dependent and silently fails on non-English systems.
questions.explanation: Post filenames give Jekyll a real Date object. A front matter value like `date: "2026-01-15"` (in quotes) stays a string; unquote it so YAML parses it as a date, or convert with `| date: "%s"` first.
